preamble: The Voice Loss of the initial palatal affricate [dʒ] (represented here as j_affricate) to its voiceless counterpart [tʃ] (ch) is a key event in the development of tonal Register Splits. Voiced affricates traditionally lowered the Pitch Contour, resulting in the Yáng (low) tonal register across East Asian languages.
phoneticEffects: Voice Loss, Devoicing, Register Split, Tonogenesis

examples.note: The historical voicing of palatal affricates in Mon-Khmer provided the low-register pitch environment necessary for the Vietnamese tonal split.
Devoicing of initial affricates led to lower-register tonal variants.
Early Middle Chinese voiced affricates (Quánzhuó) devoiced, phonemicizing the low-register pitch contours into the modern Yáng tone categories.
Initial voicing in affricates triggered tonal register differentiation.
Proto-Tai initial *dʒ contributed to the massive tonal Register Split observed in the development of modern Thai phonology.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Baxter, W. H. (1992). A Handbook of Old Chinese Phonology.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
